content.text: What Is the AWS Free Tier?

The AWS Free Tier is a set of offers that let you use certain AWS services at no cost, up to specified usage limits. It exists for two reasons: to give newcomers a risk-free way to learn and experiment with AWS services, and to allow developers to run lightweight production workloads at zero cost when they stay within the limits.

The Free Tier is not a single undifferentiated category — it contains three distinct offer types with different expiration rules, applicable services, and renewal mechanics. Conflating them is the root cause of most unexpected charges new AWS customers receive.
The Three Free Tier Categories

attrs.alt: AWS Free Tier offer types: Always Free, 12-Month Free, and Short-Term Trials

content.text: The diagram above illustrates all three categories, their representative services, and the account age timeline showing when 12-month benefits expire. The green bar spans months 1–12; after that, only Always Free limits remain active without configuration changes.
Always Free

Always Free offers never expire and are available to all AWS accounts, regardless of account age. These are services where AWS has decided the free quota supports genuine utility without cannibalizing paid usage. The key Always Free allocations are:
Service
Free Allocation
What It Supports
AWS Lambda
1M requests/mo + 400,000 GB-seconds compute/mo
Substantial serverless workloads — many small apps run entirely within this limit
Amazon DynamoDB
25 GB storage + 25 WCU + 25 RCU
A small production database — sufficient for many low-traffic applications
Amazon CloudWatch
10 custom metrics, 10 alarms, 1M API calls
Basic monitoring for a small infrastructure footprint
Amazon SNS
1M publishes, 100K HTTP deliveries, 1,000 email deliveries/mo
Notification pipelines for small-scale applications
Amazon SQS
1M requests/mo
Message queuing for lightweight async workflows
Amazon Cognito
50,000 monthly active users
Authentication for small-to-medium applications
AWS X-Ray
100,000 traces recorded, 1M traces scanned/mo
Distributed tracing for serverless or microservice apps
Amazon CloudFront
1 TB data transfer out + 10M HTTP requests/mo
CDN for sites with moderate traffic — always free, not just 12 months

The Lambda and DynamoDB Always Free quotas are particularly generous. A web application serving up to hundreds of thousands of monthly requests, backed by DynamoDB, can operate entirely within the Always Free tier indefinitely — not just for 12 months.
12-Month Free
Twelve-month free offers apply to new AWS accounts starting from the account creation date. They cover the compute and storage services most commonly used to host applications:
Service
12-Month Free Allocation
Notes
Amazon EC2
750 hours/mo of t2.micro (or t3.micro in regions without t2)
Enough for one instance running 24/7 — multiple instances share the hour pool
Amazon S3
5 GB Standard storage + 20,000 GET + 2,000 PUT requests/mo
Small static site hosting or object storage
Amazon RDS
750 hours/mo of db.t2.micro (MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, SQL Server SE, Oracle BYOL)
One database instance running 24/7; stops when account hits 12 months
Amazon CloudFront
1 TB data transfer out + 10M HTTP requests/mo
Now also Always Free — early accounts got it as a 12-month benefit
Elastic Load Balancing
750 hours/mo of Classic or Application load balancers
One ALB or CLB running continuously
Amazon EBS
30 GB of General Purpose (SSD) or Magnetic storage
Root volume for EC2 instances
Amazon ElastiCache
750 hours/mo of cache.t2.micro nodes
Redis or Memcached node for caching
AWS Elastic Beanstalk
No additional charge for Elastic Beanstalk itself
Underlying resources (EC2, ELB, S3) consume their respective free tier allocations

A critical detail with EC2: the 750 hours per month is a pool shared across all running instances of the eligible instance type. If you run two t2.micro instances simultaneously, you consume 2 hours of quota per real-time hour, exhausting 744-hour-month quota in 15 days. One instance running continuously consumes exactly 720–744 hours depending on the month — safely within the limit.
Short-Term Trials

Trial offers are service-specific and begin from the first time you use that particular service, not from account creation. They are designed to let you evaluate higher-cost managed services before committing:
Amazon SageMaker: 2 months free on a set of instance hours for ML training and hosting
Amazon Redshift: 2 months free on a dc2.large node — enough to evaluate a data warehouse workload
Amazon GuardDuty: 30-day trial for threat detection — starts the day you enable it in a region
Amazon Inspector: 15-day trial for vulnerability scanning across EC2 and container images
Amazon Macie: 30-day trial for S3 data classification and sensitive data discovery
AWS Security Hub: 30-day trial per account per region
AWS Config: 30-day trial on configuration recording
Amazon Detective: 30-day trial for security investigation

An important consequence of the 'starts on first use' rule: if you enable GuardDuty in us-east-1 today and in eu-west-1 in three weeks, your GuardDuty trial periods are different for each region. This is per-region, not per-account.
Free Tier Scope: What It Covers and What It Does Not
The Free Tier covers usage charges — the on-demand cost for running a resource. It does not eliminate all potential costs, and several cost categories fall outside Free Tier coverage entirely:
Always Charged Regardless of Free Tier
Data transfer out to the internet:

AWS charges for outbound data transfer above 1 GB per month, even during the 12-month period. CloudFront gets 1 TB free, but EC2 and S3 data transfer out beyond 1 GB per month is charged. Data transfer into AWS is always free.
Route 53 hosted zones:
$0.50 per hosted zone per month is charged from the first dollar — there is no Route 53 free tier for hosted zones (though DNS queries have a small free allocation).
Elastic IP addresses:
An Elastic IP that is not attached to a running instance is charged $0.005/hour. This catches people who allocate an EIP and then stop their instance — the address is now wasted and billing starts.
Snapshots and backups:
EBS snapshots, RDS automated backup storage beyond the database size, and Glacier storage beyond the 10 GB Always Free limit are charged at standard rates.
Premium support plans:
Developer, Business, and Enterprise support plans are always charged. Only Basic support is free.
Marketplace software:
Software purchased through AWS Marketplace often carries additional license fees on top of the underlying infrastructure costs.
Monitoring Your Free Tier Usage
AWS provides several tools to monitor Free Tier consumption. Using them proactively prevents surprise bills — AWS will continue charging once you exceed limits without any automated stop.
Free Tier Usage Alerts via Email

AWS automatically sends an email alert when you exceed 85% of any Free Tier service limit. This alert goes to the root user email address by default. You can also configure additional alert recipients in the Billing and Cost Management console under Billing Preferences → Receive AWS Free Tier usage alerts.

The 85% alert gives you time to act before you start incurring charges, but the lag between actual usage and the alert can be several hours. Do not rely on the email alert as your only monitoring — combine it with proactive dashboard review.
Billing Dashboard and Free Tier Usage Tab

The AWS Billing and Cost Management console has a dedicated Free Tier tab that shows your current month's consumption for every service that has a Free Tier allocation. It displays usage as a percentage of limit, so you can immediately see which services are approaching exhaustion.
Navigate to: AWS Console → Billing and Cost Management → Free Tier. The table updates daily — not in real time — so same-day usage may not appear immediately.
AWS Budgets for Zero-Dollar Protection

AWS Budgets can be set to alert you when your monthly charges exceed any threshold. For Free Tier protection, the recommended pattern is a $0 cost budget that alerts immediately when any non-free charge appears:

The minimum budget amount is $0.01 — you cannot set $0.00, but $0.01 effectively triggers the moment any real charge accumulates. With Budgets, any breach of the Free Tier will generate an immediate email rather than waiting for the end-of-month invoice.
CloudWatch Billing Alarm
A more granular approach is a CloudWatch alarm on the
AWS/Billing
namespace metric
EstimatedCharges
. This requires enabling billing alerts in the Billing Preferences console first:

# Enable billing alerts (one-time per account, must be root or billing admin)
aws ce put-anomaly-monitor \
  --anomaly-monitor '{
    "MonitorName": "FreeTierBillingMonitor",
    "MonitorType": "DIMENSIONAL",
    "MonitorDimension": "SERVICE"
  }'

# Create a CloudWatch alarm on EstimatedCharges (must be in us-east-1 — billing metrics only exist there)
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --alarm-name "FreeTierBreach" \
  --alarm-description "Alert when monthly charges exceed $1" \
  --metric-name EstimatedCharges \
  --namespace AWS/Billing \
  --statistic Maximum \
  --dimensions Name=Currency,Value=USD \
  --period 86400 \
  --evaluation-periods 1 \
  --threshold 1.0 \
  --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold \
  --alarm-actions arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:ACCOUNT_ID:billing-alerts
Important:
Billing metrics are only available in
us-east-1
. The CloudWatch alarm must be created in that region regardless of where your resources run.
Common Free Tier Traps and How to Avoid Them

New AWS users consistently encounter the same unexpected charges. Understanding these patterns prevents the frustration of receiving an unexpected invoice after assuming everything was within the free tier.
Multiple EC2 Instances Consuming the Hour Pool

The 750 EC2 hours per month is shared across all running t2.micro or t3.micro instances. Launching a second instance for a second project doubles your hourly consumption rate. At 2 instances running continuously, you exhaust 744 of your 750 hours by month end, but if either instance ran for even one additional hour (which happens with any variation in launch timing), you are billed for that overage.

The safe practice: run one Free Tier–eligible instance at a time. If you want a second environment, stop the first instance. Stopped instances do not consume EC2 hours (but still consume EBS storage allocation).
RDS Incurs Charges Even When the Database Is Idle

An RDS database instance that is running but receiving no queries still consumes its 750 monthly hours. You cannot reduce RDS costs by reducing query volume — only by stopping the instance. Unlike EC2, RDS instances can be stopped manually, but AWS automatically restarts stopped RDS instances after 7 days. This means a forgotten stopped RDS instance will restart and begin consuming hours without any user action.

For development workloads, prefer storing data in DynamoDB (Always Free, no instance concept) or using Aurora Serverless v2, which can scale to zero when idle. For a traditional SQL database that you only need occasionally, remember to stop it after use and anticipate the 7-day auto-restart behavior.
EBS Snapshots Are Not Covered by the 30 GB EBS Allocation

The 30 GB Free Tier covers only active EBS volume storage. EBS snapshots — incremental backups of volumes — are stored in Amazon S3 and billed separately at standard S3 storage rates (approximately $0.05/GB/month in us-east-1). An EC2 instance with a 20 GB root volume is within the Free Tier, but if you create a snapshot of that instance's volume, you are now paying for the snapshot storage on top of the volume cost.

To avoid this: only take snapshots when genuinely needed, delete old snapshots when they are no longer needed, and understand that the Data Lifecycle Manager (which automates snapshot creation) can rapidly accumulate costs if configured without a deletion policy.
Outbound Data Transfer Charges

Data transferred from EC2, S3, or other AWS services out to the internet is free for the first 1 GB per month, then charged at approximately $0.09/GB (varying by region). Serving static assets from S3, streaming video, or hosting a high-traffic website can generate data transfer charges quickly even if all other resource usage is within Free Tier limits.

Mitigation strategies: serve static content through CloudFront (which has its own generous Always Free transfer allocation), keep large file downloads behind authentication to control bandwidth, and monitor the
DataTransfer-Out-Bytes
dimension in CloudWatch or Cost Explorer.
Multi-Region Resource Proliferation

Free Tier limits apply per account, not per region. Creating resources in multiple regions does not multiply your free allocation — it divides it. Two EC2 instances (one in us-east-1 and one in eu-west-1) consume the shared 750-hour monthly pool together, not independently.

New users experimenting across multiple regions frequently create resources and forget to clean them up, resulting in charges from resources in regions they are not actively using. Use the AWS Console's resource grouping features or run a periodic audit to find resources in unexpected regions:

# List EC2 instances across all regions
for region in $(aws ec2 describe-regions --query 'Regions[*].RegionName' --output text); do
  count=$(aws ec2 describe-instances --region "$region" \
    --filters "Name=instance-state-name,Values=running,stopped" \
    --query 'length(Reservations[*].Instances[*])' --output text 2>/dev/null || echo 0)
  if [ "$count" -gt 0 ]; then
    echo "$region: $count instance(s)"
  fi
done
Free Tier After 12 Months

When the 12-month period expires, AWS does not send a prominent warning email — there is no billing pause or grace period. On the first day of month 13, any resources that were consuming 12-month free allocations begin generating charges at on-demand rates.
Common services to review before month 13:
EC2 instances:

A t2.micro running continuously becomes approximately $8–9/month (us-east-1). If you still need it, consider a Reserved Instance (1-year no-upfront) at roughly $5/month, or Savings Plans for even lower rates.
RDS instances:
A db.t2.micro RDS instance becomes approximately $15–25/month depending on engine and region. Evaluate whether you need RDS or whether DynamoDB (Always Free) can serve the same purpose.
ELB:
Application Load Balancers become approximately $16–20/month at baseline. For simple workloads, accessing EC2 instances directly or using CloudFront as a proxy eliminates this cost.
ElastiCache:
A cache.t2.micro node becomes approximately $12/month. Evaluate whether the cache is essential or whether application-level caching suffices.

The Always Free tier — Lambda, DynamoDB, CloudWatch, SNS, SQS, Cognito — continues without change after month 12. Architectures built primarily on serverless services continue to operate at no cost even after the 12-month window closes.
Designing a Zero-Cost Learning Architecture
The Free Tier's Always Free components enable a full serverless web application that runs indefinitely at no cost. A practical example architecture:
Front end:

Static website hosted on Amazon S3 (5 GB free, 12 months) with CloudFront distribution (1 TB transfer always free). After month 12, S3 hosting charges begin — migrate to static hosting on a free CDN tier or ensure storage stays minimal.
API:
AWS Lambda for all compute (1M requests + 400K GB-seconds always free). API Gateway has a 1M API call always free allocation.
Database:
Amazon DynamoDB (25 GB + 25 WCU + 25 RCU always free) for application data storage.
Authentication:
Amazon Cognito (50K MAU always free) for user sign-up, login, and JWT token issuance.
Notifications:
Amazon SNS (1M publishes always free) for event-driven notifications.
Monitoring:
CloudWatch (10 custom metrics + 10 alarms always free) with a Budgets alert at $0.01 threshold.

This architecture can serve thousands of monthly active users within the Always Free limits as long as Lambda execution times are kept short and DynamoDB request patterns stay under the provisioned capacity. It is not just a toy — it is the foundation of many real production applications that legitimately pay $0 per month.
Free Tier Eligibility by Account Type

Not every AWS account receives the same Free Tier treatment. Understanding eligibility is important particularly in organizational contexts where accounts may be created through AWS Organizations rather than directly through the AWS website.
Standard Accounts

When you create a new AWS account directly at aws.amazon.com, the 12-month free period begins on the date of account creation. The Always Free and trial offers are also available immediately. This is the scenario most learners and individual developers encounter.
Accounts Created Through AWS Organizations
When an AWS Organizations management account creates a member account (via the Organizations console or the
aws organizations create-account

API), the new account receives a fresh 12-month Free Tier period starting from its creation date. Each account in an organization has its own independent Free Tier eligibility — being part of an organization does not affect this.

However, organizations that use consolidated billing share Service Control Policies (SCPs) and other organizational guardrails, which can restrict what services member accounts can use. An SCP that denies EC2 usage in a member account would prevent the account from utilizing its EC2 Free Tier allocation, even if the allocation exists. Free Tier eligibility and organizational policies are independent — the former is an AWS offer, the latter is your organization's configuration.
AWS Activate for Startups

AWS Activate is a separate program that provides startups with AWS credits in addition to — not instead of — the standard Free Tier. Startups that receive AWS Activate credits can apply them to any AWS usage, including usage that would otherwise exceed Free Tier limits. Credits are consumed before charges are applied, so an account with $5,000 in Activate credits will see $0 charges until those credits are exhausted.

AWS Educate and AWS Academy are analogous programs for students and educational institutions, providing access to AWS services in sandboxed environments with preconfigured resource limits. These environments sometimes restrict which services are available — the Free Tier does not apply to these sandboxed environments in the traditional sense.
Free Tier Usage Patterns by Use Case
Knowing the raw limits is useful, but understanding how they map to specific use cases makes it practical to plan what you can build without incurring costs.
Personal Portfolio Website

A static portfolio website — HTML, CSS, JavaScript — fits entirely within the Free Tier using S3 static website hosting and CloudFront as the CDN. With the 12-month free S3 allocation and the Always Free CloudFront limit, a portfolio receiving several thousand monthly visitors generates zero AWS charges for the first year and low costs (primarily S3 storage at $0.023/GB/month) after month 12.

The domain name registration (Route 53 or an external registrar) and the Route 53 hosted zone ($0.50/month) are costs outside the Free Tier, but these are minimal fixed costs rather than variable charges.
API Backend with Serverless Architecture

A REST API backend built with API Gateway + Lambda + DynamoDB can handle substantial traffic within the Always Free limits. Lambda's 1 million request free allocation is per month. At a typical request rate of 10 requests per second during business hours (roughly 200,000 requests per month), the Lambda allocation covers 5 such applications before costs begin. DynamoDB's 25 read and write capacity units support several thousand requests per minute for simple key-based lookups.

The main constraint at scale is not the request count but the compute time. Lambda bills in 1ms increments after the 400,000 GB-second allocation. A function using 512 MB of memory has a GB-second ratio of 0.5, meaning 800,000 seconds of execution time before billing begins. If each request takes 50ms, that is 16 million requests before cost — well beyond the 1M request free limit.
Learning and Certification Preparation
AWS certification exams test practical knowledge of services. The Free Tier lets candidates experiment hands-on with the services they are studying without budget risk. Recommended study patterns:
IAM experiments:
IAM has no cost at all (it is never charged separately). Create users, groups, roles, and policies freely without any budget concern.
EC2 basics:
Launch a t2.micro or t3.micro to practice SSH, security groups, user data scripts, and instance metadata. Stop it when not actively practicing — stopped instances do not consume hours.
S3 labs:
Create buckets, experiment with bucket policies, versioning, and lifecycle rules. S3 storage is minimal at 5 GB free, and operations (PUT/GET) are in the millions range.
CloudFormation:
Template authoring has no cost. Only the resources created by the template consume Free Tier. Delete stacks when finished to release resources back into the free pool.
Lambda:
Write and invoke Lambda functions freely. With 1 million free requests per month, you can invoke a function 30,000 times per day without any charges.
Small Production Workloads

The Always Free tier is genuinely production-capable for small applications. A SaaS product with under a few thousand monthly active users, where business logic runs in Lambda and data lives in DynamoDB, can remain at $0 per month indefinitely — not just during an evaluation period. Multiple startups have launched and remained in free tier through initial traction phases before graduating to paid usage.

The boundary condition is the DynamoDB capacity: 25 WCU (write capacity units) and 25 RCU (read capacity units) on provisioned capacity. At standard DynamoDB provisioned throughput, this supports roughly 25 writes per second and 25 strongly consistent reads per second — sufficient for a lightly used production application. Burst traffic beyond these rates either throttles requests or, if using on-demand capacity mode, incurs per-request charges.
Programmatic Free Tier Monitoring
While the console provides a visual Free Tier dashboard, programmatic monitoring is more reliable for catching issues outside business hours and for organizations managing multiple accounts.
Querying Free Tier Usage via the AWS API
AWS Cost Explorer has a GetRightsizingRecommendation API, but for Free Tier specifically, the most direct approach is the Cost and Usage Report (CUR) and the free-tier-specific Budgets action:
Automated Monthly Cleanup Script
A proactive defense against unexpected charges is a cleanup script run monthly before the billing period resets. This script identifies common charge sources that are easy to forget:

#!/bin/bash
# Free Tier cleanup audit — run before month end
set -euo pipefail

echo "=== EBS Snapshots (charged separately from volume allocation) ==="
aws ec2 describe-snapshots --owner-ids self \
  --query 'Snapshots[*].[SnapshotId,VolumeSize,StartTime,Description]' \
  --output table

echo ""
echo "=== Elastic IPs not attached to instances (charged when unattached) ==="
aws ec2 describe-addresses \
  --query 'Addresses[?!InstanceId].[PublicIp,AllocationId]' \
  --output table

echo ""
echo "=== Load Balancers (750h free/mo for 12 months — check if still needed) ==="
aws elbv2 describe-load-balancers \
  --query 'LoadBalancers[*].[LoadBalancerName,State.Code,CreatedTime]' \
  --output table

echo ""
echo "=== RDS Instances (running or stopped — stopped restarts after 7 days) ==="
aws rds describe-db-instances \
  --query 'DBInstances[*].[DBInstanceIdentifier,DBInstanceStatus,DBInstanceClass]' \
  --output table

echo ""
echo "=== EC2 Instances across this region ==="
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --filters "Name=instance-state-name,Values=running,stopped" \
  --query 'Reservations[*].Instances[*].[InstanceId,InstanceType,State.Name,LaunchTime]' \
  --output table

Running this audit at the beginning of each month takes under a minute and surfaces the resources most likely to generate unexpected charges. Resources listed in the output should be either intentionally retained (and budgeted for) or deleted.
Free Tier vs Spot Instances and Reserved Instances

A common question is how the Free Tier relates to other AWS pricing models like Reserved Instances, Spot Instances, and Savings Plans. The answer is that these are independent pricing mechanisms that apply at different stages.

The Free Tier applies first: if your usage is within Free Tier limits, the cost is $0 regardless of which pricing model you are using. Reserved Instances purchased for a t2.micro instance in us-east-1 provide no additional benefit if the account is already within the 12-month Free Tier EC2 allocation — the Free Tier already makes that usage $0.

After the Free Tier expires or is exhausted, pricing models kick in. A Reserved Instance for a t2.micro becomes advantageous only in month 13 and beyond when the 12-month free allocation is gone. Purchasing a Reserved Instance during the free tier period wastes money on the RI commitment since the on-demand Free Tier already covers the cost.

Spot Instances are generally not covered by the Free Tier. Even t2.micro Spot Instances may incur charges if bid prices are met. Spot is a pricing model for interruptible workloads, not a free tier mechanism, and should only be used intentionally for fault-tolerant batch jobs or flexible compute, not as a way to get free usage.
Practical rule:

Do not purchase Reserved Instances until you have exhausted your 12-month Free Tier window. Use on-demand during the free period, then evaluate Reserved Instances or Savings Plans in month 12 to prepare for paid usage in month 13.
Summary

The AWS Free Tier has three distinct categories: Always Free (never expires, for services like Lambda and DynamoDB), 12-Month Free (for compute and storage services like EC2 and RDS, starting from account creation), and Short-Term Trials (service-specific evaluation periods that begin on first use). Understanding which category applies to each service you use determines whether you will receive a bill.

Protect yourself from unexpected charges by setting a $0.01 AWS Budgets alert immediately after account creation, monitoring the Billing Dashboard's Free Tier Usage tab, and understanding the specific traps: EC2 hour pooling across instances, RDS charging even when idle, EBS snapshot storage being separate from volume allocation, and the absence of any warning when the 12-month window expires. Architectures built on Always Free serverless services (Lambda, DynamoDB, Cognito) can run indefinitely at no cost and are the most durable foundation for learning and lightweight production workloads.
